title: Should governments tax or slow AI-driven automation to protect jobs, or let labor markets adjust?
subtitle: A five-lens Council Mode run with live web retrieval, scoped source claims, explicit contradictions, and deterministic verification.
audience: Government economic-policy teams and labor-market policy advisers

status.headline: Live retrieval performed; broad AI slowdown evidence remains insufficient.
status.detail: Web search and direct official URLs were retrieved in this run. Deterministic verification passed with caveats because several contradictions remain open and some publisher full text was limited to abstract-level evidence. Publication identity was not checked with the optional metadata adapters.

bottom_line: Do not use a blanket AI automation tax or broad slowdown as the default jobs policy, and do not rely on laissez-faire adjustment. Use a targeted transition compact: tax-neutrality reform, worker support, monitoring, and sector-specific safeguards where evidence warrants them.

strongest_findings.text: Automation can harm exposed workers and local labor markets, so pure laissez-faire is too weak.

supported_by.note: Robot evidence plus institutional policy analysis.

challenged_by.note: Needs AI-specific measurement before broad intervention.

strongest_findings.text: AI exposure is large but should not be treated as realized job loss.

supported_by.note: IMF, ILO, and OECD sources preserve this distinction.

strongest_findings.text: The retrieved fiscal evidence does not support a blanket AI-specific tax as the default instrument.

supported_by.note: IMF fiscal guidance, task heterogeneity, and run-local negative evidence.

challenged_by.note: Robot-tax theory supports a narrower temporary case under assumptions.

strongest_findings.text: A targeted transition compact is better supported than either broad slowing or passive adjustment.

supported_by.note: Converges across fiscal, implementation, and historical lenses.

lens_snapshot.title: Lens posture snapshot
lens_snapshot.summary: Skeptic and practitioner pressure against a blanket tax is high; academic and historian lenses keep worker-protection urgency visible.
lens_snapshot.scale_label: 0 low posture intensity - 1 high posture intensity

lenses.name: academic
lenses.note: Supports worker risk but rejects exposure-as-loss.

lenses.name: economist
lenses.note: Rejects blunt AI tax but keeps narrow reform live.

lenses.name: practitioner
lenses.note: Tax base must distinguish substitution from augmentation.

lenses.name: historian
lenses.note: Adaptation is real but not painless.

lenses.name: skeptic
lenses.note: No evaluated broad AI tax was retrieved.

contradictions.stake: Broad AI tax versus targeted tax reform
High AI exposure versus limited current observed disruption
Administrative feasibility of taxing AI automation
Robot-tax theory versus AI-specific fiscal guidance
Evidence for an implemented broad AI slowdown

options.name: A - Blanket AI automation tax or broad slowdown
points: May visibly respond to job fears.
Weak retrieved evidence and serious targeting risk.
options.when: Only after evaluated evidence and a clear tax base exist.

options.name: B - Let labor markets adjust with minimal intervention
points: Under-protects workers and regions exposed to displacement.
options.when: Only if monitoring shows low displacement and strong wage growth in exposed occupations.

options.name: C - Targeted transition compact
points: Requires sustained fiscal and administrative capacity.
options.when: Default recommendation for a national policy team.

options.name: D - Narrow temporary tax in defined sectors
points: Can address a specific distortion or displacement channel.
Must be scoped, measured, and sunsetted.
options.when: Only for sectors with clear substitution evidence and incidence analysis.

next_actions.text: Audit the tax code for capital-labor neutrality before proposing an AI-specific tax.
Fund unemployment insurance, wage insurance pilots, labor-market programs, and training.
Build a worker-level monitoring panel for AI adoption, task change, wages, displacement, and regions.
Require any automation-tax proposal to define the taxable event, incidence, exemptions, and sunset test.
Reserve slowing tools for sector-specific safety, market-power, procurement, or displacement evidence.

gaps.text: No evaluated primary source was retrieved showing that a broad AI automation tax protected jobs in practice.
No country-specific tax-base design in this run separated substitution from augmentation at legislative precision.
No worker-level panel evidence in this run establishes medium-term AI displacement by occupation and region.

sources.title: Robots and Jobs: Evidence from US Labor Markets
sources.note: Primary empirical evidence on industrial robot exposure, employment, and wages in US commuting zones.

sources.title: Automation and New Tasks: How Technology Displaces and Reinstates Labor
sources.note: Task-based framing for displacement, reinstatement, productivity, and labor-demand effects.

sources.title: Gen-AI: Artificial Intelligence and the Future of Work
sources.note: Exposure estimates and policy framing for AI effects across advanced and emerging economies.

sources.title: Generative AI and Jobs: A Global Analysis of Potential Effects on Job Quantity and Quality
sources.note: Global task exposure analysis distinguishing augmentation potential from automation potential.

sources.title: OECD Employment Outlook 2023: Artificial Intelligence and the Labour Market
sources.note: Labor-market evidence and uncertainty around AI adoption, job quality, and worker voice.

sources.title: Broadening the Gains from Generative AI: The Role of Fiscal Policies
sources.note: Direct policy guidance on AI taxes, social protection, education, and tax-system neutrality.

sources.title: Does the U.S. Tax Code Favor Automation?
sources.note: Analyzes labor-capital tax asymmetry and automation incentives.

sources.title: Should Robots Be Taxed?
sources.note: Formal model of robot taxation and transition dynamics for routine workers.

sources.title: The Work of the Future: Building Better Jobs in an Age of Intelligent Machines
sources.note: Historical and policy context on technology, new work, and institutions.

sources.title: Evaluating the Impact of AI on the Labor Market: Current State of Affairs
sources.note: Near-term tracking of observed labor-market disruption after ChatGPT adoption.

sources.title: Technology and Jobs: A Systematic Literature Review
sources.note: Systematic review evidence on technology, displacement, reinstatement, and distributional effects.

sources.title: Live web retrieval log for Storm Council AI jobs policy run
sources.note: Records searches for evaluated AI-specific automation taxes and direct job-protection results.

minor: Open contradictions remain for human review: X-002, X-005, X-007, X-008
Contradictions marked resolved without an evidence/move basis (not counted as handled): X-006
